\begin{document}

\title{Metadata-as-Data in Apache Hudi: A Multi-Modal Index Substrate for Lakehouse Tables}

% TODO: Replace with the final author list (single-anonymous: include authors + affiliations).
\author{First Author}
\affiliation{%
  \institution{Apache Hudi (PMC), Organization}
  \city{City}
  \country{Country}
}
\email{first.author@domain.com}

\author{Second Author}
\affiliation{%
  \institution{Organization}
  \city{City}
  \country{Country}
}
\email{second.author@domain.com}

\begin{abstract}
As lakehouse tables scale toward millions of files and partitions, metadata itself becomes big data.
Traditional big data systems try to reduce metadata to keep management tractable, compromising query performance; cloud data warehouses like Snowflake, BigQuery, and Redshift instead treat metadata as a managed, indexed structure---but within proprietary architectures.
Apache Hudi brings this principle to the open lakehouse, treating \emph{metadata as data}: an internal Merge-on-Read table whose partitions implement a multi-modal indexing subsystem---column statistics, bloom filters, record-level, secondary, and expression indexes---all transactionally coupled with data commits and stored in an SSTable-like layout (HFile) optimized for the point and prefix lookups that dominate query planning.
Concurrent indexing via a three-phase protocol backfills large indexes online while writers continue to commit.
We summarize the design and report improvements on both synthetic benchmarks (TPC-DS) and upsert-heavy workloads, demonstrating 90\% reduction in data scanned (secondary index on TPC-DS SF-1000), 72\% lower write latency and 92\% less data shuffled (record-level index on a 1\,TB upsert workload), with per-key lookup latency of 1--2\,ms at Uber's 350\,PB production scale.
\end{abstract}

\keywords{Table formats, metadata management, adaptive indexing, point and range lookups, cloud object storage, Apache Hudi}

\maketitle

\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:intro}

Open table formats have become the transactional layer for lakehouse deployments on cloud object storage \cite{zaharia2021lakehouse}.
At large scale, metadata becomes the bottleneck: directory listings and bulk footer/stat reads dominate planning latency and API cost \cite{armbrust2020deltalake}.
Production deployments underscore the magnitude: Uber manages 19,500+ datasets (350\,PB, 6\,trillion rows/day) on Apache Hudi \cite{uber-hudi-blog}, and ByteDance operates an exabyte-scale lake \cite{bytedance-hudi-blog}.
As systems reach this scale, metadata requires database-style techniques for storage, indexing, and maintenance \cite{edara2021bigmetadata}.

In-file statistics such as Parquet column-chunk min/max values are necessary but insufficient: the total footer metadata is $O(\#\textit{files} \times \#\textit{columns})$, and every footer must be opened to perform file selection \cite{zeng2024columnar}.
For wide tables with thousands of columns, this amounts to hundreds of megabytes of metadata parsing per planning cycle.
External index structures that decouple file selection from footer reads are essential \cite{liu2023openformats}.

Apache Hudi addresses this by treating metadata as first-class data.
Cloud data warehouses have long recognized the value of managed metadata: Snowflake maintains per-micro-partition zone maps in a dedicated metadata service \cite{dageville2016snowflake}, BigQuery stores fine-grained column and block statistics as distributed system tables \cite{edara2021bigmetadata}, and Redshift keeps in-memory zone maps with automatic sort-key optimization \cite{armenatzoglou2022redshift}, but these are proprietary and engine-specific.
Hudi brings this principle to the open lakehouse: each table maintains an internal \emph{metadata table}, co-located with data files, that stores file listings and multiple index modalities---replacing recursive listings and broad footer scans with index-based planning \cite{hudi-metadata,onehouse-mmi}.
Hudi further supports \emph{concurrent indexing} to build large indexes online without pausing ingestion \cite{hudi-indexing}.

\textbf{Contributions.}\\
(1) Hudi's metadata-as-data design: an internal Hudi table that functions as an extensible multi-modal index substrate, with HFile-based layout and composite prefix keys (\S\ref{sec:mdt}).\\
(2) Multi-modal indexing modalities (record-level, secondary, expression, bloom) materialized as independently managed partitions (\S\ref{sec:modalities}).\\
(3) A concurrent indexing protocol that builds indexes online without pausing writers (\S\ref{sec:concurrent}).\\
(4) Evaluation on TPC-DS (secondary index) and upsert-heavy workloads (record-level index), with production evidence from Uber-scale deployments (\S\ref{sec:eval}).

\section{The Metadata Table}
\label{sec:mdt}

\subsection{Organization}
The metadata table is a Merge-on-Read (MoR) Hudi table where each metadata or index type is a distinct partition (see Table~\ref{tab:modalities}) \cite{hudi-metadata,hudi-indexes}.
MoR is the natural fit because every data commit generates metadata updates; rather than rewriting base files on each commit, MoR appends delta logs and compacts in the background, ensuring that ingestion latency is not penalized by metadata maintenance \cite{hudi-metadata}.

\subsection{Transactional consistency}
Hudi translates each data table write into metadata table updates and couples the two timelines so they remain in sync (Figure~\ref{fig:mdt-partitions-keys}) \cite{hudi-metadata}.
A write is visible only when \emph{both} the data table and metadata table are committed, preventing partial exposure of data or index state under failures \cite{onehouse-mmi}.

% --- Figure: Metadata-as-data architecture with index partitions ---
\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=4mm and 6mm]
    % --- Actor nodes ---
    \node[actor] (writer) {Writer};
    \node[actor, right=36mm of writer] (planner) {Query Planner};

    % --- Data Table (containing box with title + file types) ---
    \node[tablebox, minimum height=12mm,
      below=6mm of $(writer.south)!0.5!(planner.south)$] (dt) {};
    \node[font=\sffamily\scriptsize\bfseries, anchor=north]
      at ([yshift=-1pt]dt.north) {Data Table};
    \node[partition, anchor=east, yshift=-2mm] at (dt.center)
      (fg1) {Parquet base files};
    \node[partition, right=2mm of fg1] (fg2) {delta logs};
    \node[font=\sffamily\tiny, right=1.5mm of fg2] {\dots};

    % --- Metadata Table (containing box with title + 2x3 partition grid) ---
    \node[tablebox, minimum height=28mm, below=8mm of dt] (mdt) {};
    \node[font=\sffamily\scriptsize\bfseries, anchor=north]
      at ([yshift=-1pt]mdt.north)
      {Metadata Table\enspace{\normalfont\sffamily\tiny(internal MoR; HFile layout)}};

    % Left column: planning/pruning
    \node[partition, anchor=north west, minimum width=0.40\columnwidth]
      at ([xshift=2mm,yshift=-5mm]mdt.north west) (files)
      {\shortstack[l]{\textbf{files}\\[-1pt]\scriptsize partition $\rightarrow$ file list}};

    \node[partition, below=2mm of files, minimum width=0.40\columnwidth] (cstats)
      {\shortstack[l]{\textbf{column\_stats}\\[-1pt]\scriptsize colName/partition/file $\rightarrow$ min,max,...}};

    \node[partition, below=2mm of cstats, minimum width=0.40\columnwidth] (pstats)
      {\shortstack[l]{\textbf{partition\_stats}\\[-1pt]\scriptsize partition $\rightarrow$ agg.\ stats}};

    % Right column: writes/point lookups
    \node[partition, anchor=north east, minimum width=0.40\columnwidth]
      at ([xshift=-2mm,yshift=-5mm]mdt.north east) (bf)
      {\shortstack[l]{\textbf{bloom\_filters}\\[-1pt]\scriptsize partition,fileGroup $\rightarrow$ bloom}};

    \node[partition, below=2mm of bf, minimum width=0.40\columnwidth] (rli)
      {\shortstack[l]{\textbf{record\_index (RLI)}\\[-1pt]\scriptsize recordKey $\rightarrow$ fileGroup}};

    \node[partition, below=2mm of rli, minimum width=0.40\columnwidth] (sidx)
      {\shortstack[l]{\textbf{secondary/expr index}\\[-1pt]\scriptsize secondaryVal $\rightarrow$ recordKey(s)}};

    % --- Write path (left side) ---
    \draw[arr] (writer.south) -- (writer.south |- dt.north)
      node[lbl, right, pos=0.45] {commit};
    \draw[darr] ([xshift=12mm]dt.south west) -- ([xshift=12mm]mdt.north west)
      node[lbl, left] {atomic};

    % --- Read path (right side) ---
    \draw[arr] (planner.south) -- (planner.south |- dt.north)
      node[lbl, right, pos=0.45] {plan};
    \draw[darr] ([xshift=-12mm]dt.south east) -- ([xshift=-12mm]mdt.north east)
      node[lbl, left] {selective lookup};
    \draw[darr] ([xshift=-10mm]mdt.north east) -- ([xshift=-10mm]dt.south east)
      node[lbl, right] {pruned files set};
  \end{tikzpicture}
  \caption{Metadata-as-data architecture and index substrate. Writers atomically commit data and metadata; query planners selectively consult metadata-table partitions for pruned file sets. Left column accelerates planning/pruning; right column accelerates writes and point lookups. Each partition uses sorted HFiles with composite prefix keys.}
  \label{fig:mdt-partitions-keys}
  \Description[Metadata-as-data architecture with index partitions]{Three-tier diagram showing Writer and Query Planner actors at top, a Data Table in the middle with Parquet base files and delta logs, and a Metadata Table at bottom with six partitions in two columns: planning/pruning (files, column\_stats, partition\_stats) on the left and writes/lookups (bloom\_filters, record\_index, secondary/expr index) on the right. Solid arrows show commit and plan paths; dashed arrows show atomic coupling, selective lookup, and pruned file-set return paths.}
\end{figure}

\subsection{HFile for point and prefix lookups}
Lakehouse metadata access is dominated by \emph{point} and \emph{prefix} lookups (e.g., ``stats for column \texttt{c} in file \texttt{f}'', ``all blooms for partition \texttt{p}'').
Hudi stores metadata partitions in an SSTable-like format (HFile), maintaining records in sorted order with a block index for point reads and range scans (Figure~\ref{fig:mdt-partitions-keys}) \cite{hudi-techspec}.
Published experiments report 10--100$\times$ lower point-lookup latency for HFile compared to Parquet/Avro at 10M-entry scale \cite{onehouse-mmi}.
At Uber, the metadata table is deployed across 90\% of production datasets; for the largest tables exceeding 300 billion rows, the record index alone is sharded into up to 10,000 HFiles \cite{uber-hudi-blog}.

\paragraph{Prefix key design for wide tables.}
For column statistics, Hudi composes record keys by concatenating \emph{column name}, \emph{partition name}, and \emph{data file name}, enabling prefix scans.
Planners issue prefix lookups only for columns referenced by a query, reducing work from $O(\#\textit{table columns})$ to $O(\#\textit{query columns})$ \cite{onehouse-mmi}.

\section{Multi-Modal Indexing}
\label{sec:modalities}

Hudi's metadata table acts as an \emph{index substrate}: index modalities are materialized as partitions and can be enabled/disabled independently as workload needs evolve \cite{hudi-metadata,hudi-indexes}.
Table~\ref{tab:modalities} summarizes key modalities and their database analogues.

\begin{table*}[t]
\caption{Index modalities as metadata-table partitions. Each modality maps to a familiar database concept and is implemented as a partition within the internal Hudi metadata table.}
\label{tab:modalities}
\centering
\scriptsize
\begin{tabular}{@{}lll@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{MDT partition / modality} & \textbf{DB analogue} & \textbf{Primary effect} \\
\midrule
\texttt{record\_index} (global RLI) & primary index & fast upserts/deletes; point lookups \\
\texttt{secondary\_index} & secondary (inverted) index & accelerate predicates on non-key columns \\
\texttt{expr\_index} & functional index & accelerate predicates on expressions; aids partition evolution \\
\texttt{column\_stats} & zone maps / min-max stats & file pruning via data skipping; wide-table selective reads \\
\texttt{partition\_stats} & aggregated stats & coarse partition pruning without file scans \\
\texttt{bloom\_filters} & membership filters & prune candidate files for existence checks / upserts \\
\texttt{files} (file listings) & catalog / manifest cache & avoid object-store listings; faster planning/writes \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table*}

\subsection{Record-level index}
The record-level index (RLI) maps record keys to data-table locations in the \texttt{record\_index} partition \cite{hudi-indexes,hudi-techspec}.
This is the lakehouse analogue of a \emph{primary index}: it enables efficient upserts and deletes by avoiding full file-group scans to locate candidate records.
To accommodate large key spaces, the record index uses hash-based sharding and supports point lookups for keyed reads \cite{hudi-indexes,hudi-rli-blog}.
We evaluate RLI performance on upsert workloads in \S\ref{sec:eval}.

\subsection{Column statistics and data skipping}
The \texttt{column\_stats} partition stores per-file, per-column aggregates (min, max, null count, value count) in the metadata table.
Query planners evaluate predicates against these statistics to skip files whose value ranges provably exclude matching records---the same principle as zone maps in cloud data warehouses \cite{dageville2016snowflake,edara2021bigmetadata,armenatzoglou2022redshift}.
This coarse-grained pruning composes with bloom filters and secondary indexes to progressively narrow the candidate file set.

\subsection{Expression index}
An expression index indexes a function of a column, stored as an \texttt{expr\_index} partition \cite{hudi-indexes,hudi-techspec}.
This is the lakehouse analogue of indexes on expressions in PostgreSQL \cite{pg-expr-index}: it creates an access path for predicates that do not match raw columns.

\paragraph{Partition evolution.}
A table partitioned by day cannot prune at finer granularity without repartitioning; an expression index on an hour-extraction function provides hour-level pruning without changing the physical layout.

\subsection{Adaptive bloom filters}
Hudi supports \emph{adaptive bloom filters} for membership testing during upserts, adjusting filter size per file to maintain a configured false-positive ratio \cite{hudi-indexes}.
This matters for streaming and skewed workloads where file-group cardinalities vary and static filters would saturate.

\subsection{Secondary index}
The secondary index accelerates predicates on non-key columns by mapping secondary values to primary keys, then using RLI to retrieve file locations.
Its key encoding concatenates escaped secondary and primary keys, supporting uniqueness and efficient decoding \cite{hudi-techspec}.
The resulting two-hop lookup pattern---secondary value $\rightarrow$ record key $\rightarrow$ file location---composes with the existing record-level index rather than maintaining a separate file-location mapping.
We evaluate secondary index performance in \S\ref{sec:eval}.

\section{Concurrent Indexing}
\label{sec:concurrent}

Indexes increase write cost because they must be maintained as data changes.
Rather than pausing writers to build or rebuild large indexes, Hudi supports asynchronous index building and \emph{concurrent indexing} (Figure~\ref{fig:concurrent}) \cite{hudi-indexing}.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \begin{tikzpicture}[x=1mm, y=1mm]
    % --- Lane labels ---
    \node[font=\sffamily\scriptsize\bfseries, anchor=east] at (-2,12) {Writer};
    \node[font=\sffamily\scriptsize\bfseries, anchor=east] at (-1,0) {Indexer};

    % --- Writer timeline ---
    \draw[arr] (0,12) -- (72,12);

    % --- Commit markers on writer lane ---
    \foreach \x/\lab in {10/c_1, 26/c_2, 44/c_3, 60/c_4} {                  \fill (\x,12) circle[radius=0.9pt];                                   \node[font=\sffamily\tiny, above=1mm] at (\x,12) {$\lab$};
    }

    % --- Phase blocks on indexer lane ---
    % Schedule phase
    \node[phase, fill=gray!40, minimum width=12mm, anchor=west]
      (sched) at (14,0) {Schedule};
    \node[annot, below=0.5mm of sched] {lock; snapshot $c_1$};

    % Execute phase
    \node[phase, fill=gray!15, minimum width=22mm, anchor=west]             (exec) at (27,0) {Execute};                                         \node[annot, below=0.5mm of exec] {materialize (no lock)};

    % Reconcile phase
    \node[phase, fill=gray!40, minimum width=12mm, anchor=west]
      (reconcile) at (50,0) {Reconcile};
    \node[annot, below=0.5mm of reconcile] {validate $c_2$,$c_3$; lock};

    % --- Vertical dashed line at indexing instant ---
    \draw[densely dashed, thin, gray!60] (14,17) -- (14,-9);
    \node[annot, anchor=north] at (14,-8) {indexing instant};
  \end{tikzpicture}
  \caption{Concurrent indexing protocol. The indexer snapshots completed commits under a brief lock (Schedule), materializes index files while writers continue unblocked (Execute), then reconciles missed commits under a metadata-table lock (Reconcile).}
  \label{fig:concurrent}
  \Description[Concurrent indexing timeline]{Swimlane timeline with two lanes: Writer lane showing four commits (c1 through c4) on a horizontal arrow, and Indexer lane below showing three phases: Schedule (dark gray, takes brief lock at commit c1), Execute (light gray, no lock, runs while c2 and c3 are committed), and Reconcile (dark gray, validates c2 and c3 under lock). A vertical dashed line marks the indexing instant.}
\end{figure}

Concurrent indexing uses a three-phase protocol that combines optimistic and multi-version concurrency control techniques:
(1) \textbf{Schedule} creates an indexing plan over all completed commits up to an indexing instant under a brief lock;
(2) \textbf{Execute} materializes index files without holding any lock, allowing writers to continue committing; and
(3) \textbf{Reconcile} acquires the metadata-table lock, validates commits that completed during execution, and applies catch-up updates, aborting on inconsistencies.
Because metadata updates are transactionally coupled to data commits (\S\ref{sec:mdt}), this protocol can establish a consistent index snapshot while leaving writers unblocked during the expensive materialization phase.

\section{Evaluation}
\label{sec:eval}

We report published benchmark results for two index modalities and summarize production evidence from Uber-scale deployments.

\paragraph{Setup.}
Both benchmarks use Amazon EMR clusters.
The secondary index benchmark uses Hudi 1.0.1 with Spark 3.5.5 on 10 \texttt{m5.xlarge} instances \cite{hudi-secondary-index-blog}.
The RLI benchmark uses Hudi 0.14.0 with Spark 3.2.1 on 10 \texttt{m5.4xlarge} instances over a 1\,TB dataset (2 billion records) \cite{hudi-rli-blog}.

\paragraph{Secondary index.}
On TPC-DS SF-1000, a secondary index on the \texttt{ship\_customer\_sk} column in the \texttt{web\_sales} fact table reduces scanned data by 90\% and improves warm-run latency by 58\% (Table~\ref{tab:tpcds}).

\begin{table}[t]
\caption{Secondary index benchmark on TPC-DS SF-1000, \texttt{web\_sales} fact table. Results from \cite{hudi-secondary-index-blog}.}
\label{tab:tpcds}
\centering
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}lrrrr@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Setting} & \textbf{Run 1 (s)} & \textbf{Run 2 (s)} & \textbf{GB read} & \textbf{Files read} \\
\midrule
No secondary index & 32 & 14 & 67 & 5000 \\
With secondary index & 22 & 6 & 7 & 521 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\paragraph{Record-level index.}
On the 1\,TB upsert workload, RLI reduces write latency by 72\% and data shuffled during upserts by 92\% compared to a global simple index that scans all file groups (Table~\ref{tab:rli}).

\begin{table}[t]
\caption{Record-level index benchmark on 1\,TB (2B records). Results from \cite{hudi-rli-blog}.}
\label{tab:rli}
\centering
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}lrr@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Metric} & \textbf{Global Simple} & \textbf{Record Index} \\
\midrule
Write latency (relative) & 1.0$\times$ & 0.28$\times$ (\textbf{72\% reduction}) \\
Data shuffled & 85\,GB & 0.7\,GB (\textbf{92\% reduction}) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\paragraph{Production evidence.}
Of Uber's 19,500+ Hudi datasets (\S\ref{sec:intro}), 3,600 upsert-heavy tables rely on the record index in production, with per-key lookup latency of 1--2\,ms from sharded HFiles \cite{uber-hudi-blog}.

\paragraph{Limitations.}
The benchmarks above use Hudi-internal published results on different Hudi versions (0.14.0 for RLI, 1.0.1 for secondary index) and do not include a head-to-head comparison with Delta Lake or Iceberg indexing mechanisms.
Independent, version-controlled benchmarks on a shared workload suite remain important future work.

\section{Discussion}

All three major open table formats address the metadata bottleneck with different architectural choices.
Delta Lake consolidates file-level statistics in a transaction log with periodic Parquet checkpoints \cite{armbrust2020deltalake,delta-protocol}.
Iceberg organizes snapshots as a manifest hierarchy with per-file column statistics in manifests and Puffin sidecar files for bloom filters and sketches \cite{iceberg-spec}.
Both designs reduce reliance on per-file footer scans for query planning.

Hudi extends the metadata-management idea into a generalized index substrate.
Rather than a log or manifest tree, Hudi stores metadata in an internal MoR table where each index modality (record-level, secondary, expression) is an independently managed partition \cite{hudi-metadata,hudi-indexes}.
The HFile-based layout optimizes for point and prefix lookup patterns as well as fast metadata updates, and the concurrent indexing protocol (\S\ref{sec:concurrent}) provides an explicit mechanism for building indexes online \cite{hudi-indexing}.
This positions the metadata table not as a static manifest cache but as an extensible, lookup-oriented index structure that borrows from database indexing principles.
In enterprise data lakes where records flow through multiple transformation stages (raw ingestion, enrichment, curated layers) with upserts at each stage, such a substrate directly reduces the cost of locating and updating records across the pipeline.

\section{Conclusion}

At exabyte scale, metadata management is a data-management problem.
Hudi's metadata-as-data design stores metadata and indexes as a first-class internal MoR table, couples updates via multi-table transactions, and organizes index modalities as independently maintained partitions.
The metadata table storage format optimizes for the point and prefix lookup patterns that dominate lakehouse query planning, and concurrent indexing enables online backfill without pausing writers.
Future directions include bitmap and vector indexes for broader predicate types, cost-based index selection, and cross-format index interoperability.
As tables grow, metadata must be treated as managed, indexed data, and we believe the metadata-as-data pattern offers a principled foundation for this evolution.
